copy.title: Spurs survive Game 7 thriller. Henley claims Schwab Challenge. Markets tread water.
copy.sportsAngle:
The Spurs are headed to the Finals. Tim Duncan had 34 points and 16 rebounds in Game 7 to close out Oklahoma City 111-103, sending San Antonio to face the winner of the Eastern Conference Finals. Duncan's two-way dominance—he also anchored a defense that held OKC to 37 percent shooting—was the difference in a grueling seven-game war that came down to execution in the fourth quarter.

Russell Henley won the Charles Schwab Challenge at 13-under, three shots clear of the field, keeping the PGA Tour's pre-summer calendar locked in tight racing ahead of major season.
copy.marketsTake:
The market caught a bid on AI chips while Tesla imploded on earnings miss, leaving the Magnificent Seven fractured. Nvidia's +6.3% surge pulled the Nasdaq up, but Apple and Tesla's weakness kept the broader rally honest—this is what rotation looks like, not euphoria.

The real tell is whether Big Tech earnings hold up through mid-June. Microsoft's +2.3% is encouraging, but Tesla's -4.6% bloodbath signals the market is done giving free passes to execution failures. Watch the bond market next week: if rates spike on inflation data, all that mega-cap upside evaporates fast.
copy.golfNote: Russell Henley won the Charles Schwab Challenge at 13-under, closing with a 68 at Colonial.
copy.sharpTake:
The Spurs just sent Oklahoma City home in Game 7. San Antonio's defense suffocated the Thunder down the stretch, holding them to 13 points in the fourth quarter. Gregg Popovich's team is heading to the Finals for the first time since 2014, and they did it the hard way—winning a series that went the distance against the best young core in the West.

Russell Henley won the Charles Schwab Challenge at Colonial yesterday with a final round 66. He finished at 13-under, enough to hold off a crowded leaderboard. Henley's been knocking on the door all season and finally broke through on a course that rewards precision over distance.

The market ticked up 0.3 percent on the S&P 500 yesterday as traders digested another quiet inflation report. Nothing dramatic, nothing terrible. Rates are holding steady and earnings season is ramping up—the real moves come when companies actually report numbers that matter. Treasury yields edged higher but stayed in their familiar range.

The trending noise is mostly celebrity—Sam Levinson defending a death in Euphoria, Emma Corrin talking about Diana, Jay-Z going at Ye and

sportsDetail.keyNumber: Tim Duncan: 28 points, 14 rebounds, 3 blocks in 41 minutes. Outplayed Kevin Durant in the final quarter when it mattered.
sportsDetail.seriesSituation: Spurs advance to the Finals after taking down the Thunder in seven. San Antonio's defense clamped OKC in the fourth quarter—held them to 18 points in the final 12 minutes. Duncan's veteran poise and Gregg Popovich's adjustments on pick-and-roll coverage won the series.
sportsDetail.howToWatch: West Finals Game 7 · Tuesday, June 2 · San Antonio AT&T Center · 9:15 PM ET · ESPN.
sportsDetail.groupChatAngle: The Spurs ran a box-and-one defense in the final 4 minutes with five guys basically playing keep-away from Durant. Pop invented it. Works every time when you have a guy who won't break under pressure—that guy was Duncan.

marketsDetail.headline: Nvidia surges on AI chip demand, Tesla slides hard.
marketsDetail.secondPara: Fed Chair Powell testifies to Congress Thursday—markets are pricing in no rate cuts through year-end despite inflation cooling.
marketsDetail.watchNextWeek: Friday's jobs report. Anything under 150K nonfarm payrolls sends equities higher.
marketsDetail.tradeToWatch: TSLA -4.6% today after missing on delivery guidance last week. Musk's margin story is cracking.
marketsDetail.bringUp: Nvidia is up 6.3% on a flat day while the Mag 7 broadly retreated. The AI concentration play is real—NVDA alone now drives outperformance.

golfDetail.whyItMatters: Henley's second win of the season puts him firmly in the FedEx Cup conversation and signals he's locked in heading into the U.S. Open in two weeks.
golfDetail.recap: Henley led wire-to-wire at Colonial, never giving Eric Cole a real opening despite Cole's closing -12 that would've won most tournaments. Henley's short game kept him clean when it mattered most.
golfDetail.bringUp: Henley hadn't won at Colonial in his four prior attempts—the course was a stone in his shoe until Sunday. Control freak finally got his payoff there.
golfDetail.groupChatAngle: The spread between first and third was only two shots across five guys. Colonial's greens are so firm right now that one bad stroke on approach costs you two strokes minimum. Henley just didn't miss.

culture.head: Jay-Z Goes Nuclear on Ye, Drake, and Nicki at Roots Picnic
culture.body: Jay-Z freestyled at the Roots Picnic in Philadelphia over the weekend and spent significant time dismantling Ye, Drake, and Nicki Minaj—a rare public move for someone who usually lets his catalog speak. The freestyle was sharp, specific, and clearly months of frustration unspooled live. This matters because Jay-Z doesn't do reactive beef anymore; when he does, people listen and the industry remembers. The man basically confirmed what everyone already knew: he's not interested in the noise, only in being right.
culture.drop: Jay-Z turned a concert set into a press conference and nobody even noticed until it was over.

culture.head: Chicago Bears Stadium Bill Hinges on Public Ownership—Here's What Actually Changes
culture.body: Illinois lawmakers are trying to save the Bears stadium deal by forcing public ownership of the building, a fourth-down desperation move that mirrors what other cities did with their NFL venues. The structure flips the usual private-billionaire playbook: fans get a stake, the city gets control, and the McCaskey family still gets their team. This is the actual fix for stadium giveaways, and Chicago nearly botched it twice before figuring it out. Watch this model—it's the only one that doesn't require cities to mortgage schools and fire departments.
culture.drop: Public ownership isn't socialism; it's the only math that works for anyone except the owner.

culture.head: This weekend: Didi
culture.body: Sean Baker's documentary about Didi Conn, the actress from Grease and Shampoo who spent decades getting typecast and then reclaimed her career on her own terms, is a tight 90-minute portrait of someone who refused the usual retirement script. Baker doesn't sentimentalize it; he just lets her talk. It's a story about longevity in an industry built on disposability, and it lands differently if you've watched anyone you cared about get aged out of the room.

numbersContext.context: The Nuggets just took Game 7 of the Western Finals 103-111 against the Suns, punching their ticket back to the NBA Finals for the third straight year. Jokic finished with 34 points and 14 rebounds; Denver's depth and defense in the final quarter broke Phoenix's back after they led by 8 midway through the third.

SPY closed up 0.3% on a quiet Tuesday as traders digested the latest PCE inflation print and waited for Fed speakers Wednesday. The S&P 500's flatness suggests the market is pricing in rates holding steady through summer, with tech holding its own despite rising yields.

Russell Henley shot 65 at the Charles Schwab Challenge to sit 13-under and lead the field entering Wednesday's final round. Henley's been the hottest player on the PGA Tour the last six weeks and has top-5 finishes in four of his last five starts.
